speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape three of our interview with Esther Clifford. Miss Clifford, you were telling us about the train ride that seemed to go on forever. And you finally got off the train, and how did you find out where you were?

speaker: interviewee
text: It probably said Beuthen. There was-- this was a town named Beuthen, B-E-U-T-H-E-N. It was by the Polish border.

speaker: interviewer
text: Had you come on-- what kind of trains had you come on?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was not the type of train that later people were deported later on. It was a regular train, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were saying that they had you marching when you got to Beuthen and began to give orders. What kind of orders were they giving?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were ordering turn, turn left, turn right, march slow, quick, stop. There were all kinds of order. Hold up your hands. Show your identification paper. There always seems to be some kind of order that we were watching what they are going to say next.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did there seem to be any purpose to the orders?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not too me, really. When we had to walk to the right or to the left, maybe there was letting us know which way to go. But they were-- they were marching that way anyway. I don't-- I don't know if-- I really don't know if there was any purpose to it. Well, we were marching towards the Polish border. And, and then there was that time when they ordered us to stop at the last German control, the Deutsch kontrolle. And at that time, they, they ordered us to stop and hold up our hands with a certain paper that was a identification that we were supposed to have.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this an identification paper that everyone had, or only Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only-- only-- well, in this case, only polish Jews. Every Jew had to have some kind of an identification paper-- not just one, many. But the Polish Jews had-- some of them had passports. Some of them just had papers, no passport-- just identification papers. These identification papers, by the way, listed not only the first name and the last name, but the men had to use the name Israel and the women had to use Sarah. That was the middle name given by the Nazis for every Jewish person by that time.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I remember my mother had-- on mother's paper, she had Selda Sarah Ebe, and my father had Abraham Israel Eber. That was his name, and every Jew had to have that middle name. We were now stopping, and I was on my father's identification paper because I was not yet 18, or a certain age that you had to be. And I was tired. I was hungry. I was thirsty.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I think, because I'm trying to remember why I did not hold up my hands when I was ordered to do so. Either because I did not have the paper, or because I was-- for some reason, I did not. And the next thing I remember is that there was a storm trooper. The storm troopers were helping the SS with certain things. And he pointed the rifle at us, and said, get out.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my parents said, why? And just to me, I have to get out of line. And my parents said, why? And he said, because you didn't hold up your hands. You didn't hold up your arms. And they tried to explain that I was on my father's identification paper, but he just wouldn't listen.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then he lifted his rifle again, and it looked like he was going to hit us all. And because my parents-- my family was holding onto my hand, being the youngest especially, holding my hands very tightly. And as he lifted his rifle, I didn't want to get anybody hurt, so I pulled myself loose with all my might. And I remember my mother crying, said, no, stay here, and she begged with this-- was a very young storm trooper to let me stay with them. And here, I'll show you. I remember my mother said, I'll show you the paper, I'll show you she's-- my husband's identification paper. And she was tired, she was trying to-- and nothing. Then he ordered me to, to run to the wall-- to go to the wall, face the wall. There was a wall about a few feet away from that line of, of, of deported Jews. And I was not the only one. There were many others who were pulled out of line for some reason or another. At that wall, facing the wall, as I looked further, I saw young mothers with tiny newborn babies. They probably couldn't raise their hands. They had maybe-- you know, in one hand they had a baby. On the other hand, they had diapers and bottles or whatever. And so there were about maybe 50 other people pulled out of that line facing the wall. And I, I do remember thinking, oh my goodness, if they do anything to me, if they shoot me or anything, my fam-- that was what on my mind. My family was going to have to watch them shoot me. And I was almost relieved when I heard them order, march, march on. And I heard the footsteps. I couldn't look back. I had to face the wall. And then I heard them march on. They were marching on. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Just for a moment, just to clarify, which members of your family now were marched toward Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was my mother, my father, my brother, and my-- my brother Leo, and my sister Rosa. They were, the four of them, ordered to continue marching.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were still facing the wall?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was still facing the wall, right. And that took quite a while. And now I'm-- now I heard the steps behind me getting, you know, further away and further away. After a while, I could tear them march, and you could hear that they were getting not so noisy anymore. And then they ordered us to turn around, and they put us on one of these infamous trucks again. Those-- when I say us, there was about 50 people that had to face the wall, and we had to get up on the truck. And as I was standing high up on the truck, I could see from very far the last people of these deported people marching, marching away. And I was looking to see if I recognize my family, and of course, I couldn't. And the trucks brought us to a place, to a synagogue that was converted into a prison in anticipation of all the deportations that were yet to come. This was only 1938. And this was just a deportation of Polish Jews.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were in that synagogue, and they-- we were in a-- we were let out onto this yard outside the synagogue. And, and the Jewish people from that-- from Beuthen came over and gave us food. These were German Jewish people who were not deported. Only Polish people were deported. And we were young, and young people would bring us food, and so on. And it was-- I don't know when, whether it was how many days, or was it one day? Was it four days? I don't remember that.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I do remember when some of the young people-- and meanwhile, you make friends with the-- with other girls your age. I made some friends, and then, they said, why don't-- the gate is open. Just get out. And we have two cars waiting, we'll take you to the train. And we did. He was right. The gate was open, and the five of us raced out. They pushed us into the car. They took us to the railway station. But I learned later that the other people that were in that synagogue were also released only a few hours later. What I learned was that the, the, the other people-- many other Polish Jewish people were taken-- were supposed to be taken over to Poland. But they were sent back because Poland, at that time decided that they're not going to let any more Jews in. They only let in the first group of Jews. And my parents, we were amongst the very first. I think after our-- after our deportation, the people that were on that first train got through to the Polish border. After that, they closed the border. And the other-- many Polish Jews that were also deported were just sent back. They were just let go because didn't know what to do with them. And they were let go. But my parents, my sister and brother, somehow went through the one or two miles to go through. And we had no idea where they went to, and now I went-- I got on that train. I went back to Frankfurt.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were these young men from-- the young Jewish men from Beuthen that helped you to escape, did you ever know who they were, really?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I never really-- I kind of in my heart, I blessed them. But as I said, if I had waited a little bit longer, I would have gotten out anyway because all the others were sent home. As they-- as they sent back the other Polish Jews that they deported, they opened up the synagogue and let everybody else also. They just let them go. And they could do whatever they want. Of course, most of them went back where they were deported from.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do when you got back to Frankfurt?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I got back to Frankfurt, I-- the first thing I did is I had forgotten to mention that I had-- before I was deported, I worked in a store to-- I learned to make hats, to become a milliner, to make hats. And I was there about a year maybe, not quite a year, and I liked it very much. I figured, this way, I will be able to do dressmaking, and I'll be able to make hats when I-- when I emigrate. That was the only thing we had on our mind. There was nothing else on our mind, you know-- to get out of Germany, and make a life for yourself, and to work. And when I returned, the boss that I worked for-- and her name was Mrs. Friedman. I remember that. She had a millinery store, a hat store, on the main street of Frankfurt, called it Zeil. Z-E-I-L. I visit-- I went to the store, and I-- and she, she embraced me. She didn't know what to do with me because she couldn't understand why I didn't come to work until she learned that the Polish Jews were deported. And then she figured out that I must have been there also. So she was the first person I, I'd been to see.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did she help you?

speaker: interviewee
text: She gave me shelter, she let me stay in her house for a few days. And I didn't go to work because I-- at that time, I was too exhausted, and too-- and also I didn't-- I was afraid because I was supposed to be-- I was supposed to be over in Poland, or someplace. I wasn't supposed to be home. I didn't know whether-- I didn't know at the time that other Polish Jews were coming back, were sent back. You see, you didn't know what was going on. It wasn't that there was television or anything like that. So I was very much afraid, and I was hiding first in her house.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I-- and then the next thing I went back to my-- to where we lived. It was-- I think it was right-- I went-- I think it was before I even went to see her. I have to concentrate. Now, who did I see first? I don't really quite-- I think first I went to the house before I saw-- before I went to this Mrs. Friedman. And when I went to the house, the apartment was sealed. And as I waited and thinking what I should do next, a neighbor came down the stairs. And I was just standing at the door hiding, you know, trying to turn my head the other way so that he wouldn't recognize me. But he came over and he said, in a very nice way, can I be of help to you? He was a non-Jewish neighbor, Christian neighbor that has known-- had known us and before Hitler, we were friendly with him.

speaker: interviewee
text: He-- and I said to him, I just came back, and I would like to get into this apartment, but it is sealed. And he said he'll help me. He has the key. And he broke-- he must have heard me come or something and tried the door. He broke the seal, and he gave me a key. And he said, I should go in, but be very quiet so that the other neighbors wouldn't hear me in there. And try not to turn on any lights. And I said, I have no money. I had nothing on me, not even one penny. I had no bag, no nothing. And he said that if I wanted to sell him something, he'll give me some money. And I sold him some items that I saw stand-- I let him look around, and asked him what he wanted. And he saw a few things, a couple of silver things. And he gave me some money, and he left. And, and then I-- and I remember looking around, and I saw these-- my mother was trying, before we were deported, to make some hot tea for us. And there the cups were standing on the table with the-- with-- and of course, they didn't let her. So I remember seeing these cups, and I did nothing but cry. And so from then on, and then I visited this-- my-- Mrs. Friedman and visited. Then I went to her, and after that I, I stayed with some of the people because I was very much too-- very afraid to be in the house. But I, I went back. I did sleep-- I did stay in the house. In the evenings I would go back to the house because everybody was involved with their own-- especially after this Polish deportation, the other Jews became very much concerned that this will happen to them. And they were also afraid to take somebody in who was already deported. And I kind of fell asleep like in a coma in the house. And after that, I remember, I woke up one night and there was a lot of noise, like the breaking of glass. And I looked out. I looked through the blinds, and I saw that there were people throwing stones through the windows that we knew-- that I knew that Jewish people lived there. And they were throwing stones through all the windows-- bricks and all kinds of things through those windows. And I realized that they're breaking all the windows of the Jews, and that is what became the infamous Kristallnacht, crystal night. The first thing, I was hiding under the bed, and I realized I have to make my own decisions now. I have nobody anymore to tell me what to do next. It was always my parents who told us what to do. And I decided-- I looked again through the window, and I decided that they're going to come through this window, also. So I put on-- I dressed myself warm, and I ran out of the house. I was afraid-- I was always afraid to stay in the house since I was deported. And I ran out of the house, and I ran down. I ran to the next block, and there was the-- some place nearby was this synagogue-- Breuer Synagogue, spelled B-R-E-U-E-R. And that synagogue was in flames, and there were young people standing there throwing stones through these beautiful windows. It was a gorgeous synagogue. It was-- it was well known, beautiful, and it was up in flames. And I was standing there at the corner in awe. The synagogue was something-- we went to the synagogue even the recent Friday, the recent Sabbath, and now the synagogue is in flames. I couldn't get over that. But I realized very quickly that I can't push my luck, and I kept on running. I thought-- this was just taking place in our street. And I just kept on running and running any place, just to get away from this chaos. And then I realized that they were still throwing bricks in many other windows. And, and I went to the-- to this main street where we have the store. And all the-- all the windows were broken. And they had Jude written in big letters, Jude. And I went-- and I run back again. I didn't know where to run. I always thought that it was just-- there must be a place where they didn't throw these. But wherever I ran, they was throwing bricks. And at the same time, they had these infamous trucks standing, and bringing out men from houses, pulling them out and, you know, like by their neck. There was one in each hand, these, these SS men. And there were-- and young boys, too. They looked really young. But it was only men. I didn't see any women or children at that time. And as I was running and running, I was out of breath. And there was in Frankfurt, they had to have a street, but they have on one side trees and benches, and on the other side they had these houses where these well-to-do people living there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was so out of breath, I sat down on the bench thinking, what should I do? Where am I going to go now? I wasn't-- I wasn't going to go back to the house. I figured I couldn't go back to the house. And I was-- I thought, I have to-- what am I going to do now? Where am I going to go? And I was in a specifically bad situation because I didn't even know what that I was supposed to be back here. Wasn't I supposed to be at in that synagogue, or over in Poland? And I sat on the bench, and I remember looking at these nice houses, but the sky was red from all the fires, from all the synagogues that were burning. And I saw opposite in the house, there was-- windows were broken. There was a woman walking back and forth with a little child on her shoulder. And it was dark, but you could see her trying to kind of put that child-- that baby to sleep.

speaker: interviewee
text: And, and then-- and then I was looking down on the bench where I was sitting. In big letter it said, Juden verboten. Jews are not permitted to sit here. And I thought, well, I can't push my luck. And I have to get up. Somebody is going to find me on that bench. And I kept on running. But while sitting on the bench, I decided to-- I had a little money from the things, I thought, I decided to run to the railway station because my sister-- my second sister Mary was still in Munich. And I thought I'll go to Munich because this way I'll go away from here. And that's what I did. And I-- and while-- while running, I saw so many men being pulled out of the houses, and children crying. [GERMAN], you know, send my-- give me my, my, my-- give me my father back. And women crying at the windows. And then I run all along this street, Kaiserstrasse, that leads to the-- to the German railway station. And even there, Jews still had offices there, and some of them were trying to hide in their offices. But that didn't help. They were-- I saw them just running along. I saw-- by that time it was getting a little bit lighter already. I saw them pulling out Jews from these office buildings and putting-- and I saw the trucks, trucks standing all along the Kaiserstrasse. And I got to the railway station, and I got to a train. And I went-- the train took me to Munich. And I thought, oh, I-- I'm glad I'm getting away from that chaos. But when I got to Munich, the same thing was happening there. As a matter of fact, I was running to where my sister lives. She didn't know I was coming. I didn't even know if she was there. I didn't know where, if she was in the house. But I could even see, you know, the, the glass on the floor. The Jews had-- they made the Jews clean up the glass, and there were-- there was one poor Jew who was-- they were teasing him. There's a little bit of glass left, and they were standing around teasing him. Hey, you Jew, pick up, there's a little bit of glass left. Just remembering these things. And I stayed in my sister's house for a week because they were ready to go to Shanghai. They had some kind of a ticket that would take them to Shanghai, and they were packing and getting ready. And I realized that I didn't know Munich. I didn't know how to hide there. I didn't know-- so I decided after a week-- I couldn't stay much longer there anyway. They only had two tickets for her and her husband. And I went back to Frankfurt.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your sister and brother-in-law's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was my sister Mary, and her husband's name was Arthur.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Arthur Halberstad.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened when you went back to Frankfurt?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I went back to Frankfurt, I went back to the apartment and decided to pack some things. I didn't know what for, but I, I was thinking-- I had so many things in my mind. I was thinking, maybe I would pack and go to Poland, and find my parents. And so we had a crate in the house. We always had a crate, because we were always ready to leave. And I packed as much as I could into that crate, and I had it picked up by some kind of a-- some kind of a company that was very close. And they picked it up. I said, leave it there until I tell you what to do with it. But there wasn't much. I packed just a few things. And, and from then on, then you heard people being picked up, and taken into prison, and being deported. And there was all kinds of things. And I was just hiding, hiding wherever ever I could. I remembered my mother had a friend that I would go to her house one night. Sometimes I would sit up on the attic on the last floor, because I was always afraid. I could have-- I could go to the house just a little while longer. Because one day, a policeman came to the house. I was just in there, and I thought, this is the end. And he said that I have to come to the police. But he didn't take me with him, he said I have to come to the police. And I thought-- I didn't think that-- you know, that-- I really thought this is it.

speaker: interviewee
text: But when I came to police, he said, you have to sign. You have to sign your apartment. You have to sign that you're giving away your apartment to the-- to the [NON-ENGLISH] and to the-- to the parties. A social democrat to the-- not social. What kind-- what was the democrat-- what were they called? To the Nazis whatever they were called. And, and I remember, he got up and he said softly to me, he said, you know, you don't have to if you don't want to. And he walked out. And I was sitting there, wondering if I could trust him. And then I thought, but whatever am I going to do with this apartment? I have to go away. I have to go someplace. And what am-- So I was afraid, and I signed the apartment away, so to say. And from then on, I stayed with the friend that I had. I had a very good friend. Her name was Hertha Hahn. And she had a smaller brother, and her parents. They lived-- and he had been deported to Buchenwald on, on, on Kristallnacht. And they said-- and he came back somehow. He came back because he was anticipating a visa to go to England. And he said, I could stay with them, and I stayed with them. About when is this? About what month was this now?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was now-- this was now, I would say, January of '39. This was the beginning of 1939.

speaker: interviewer
text: And would you spell the family's name for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. The last name was Hahn, H-A-H-N, and my friend's name was Hertha, H-E-R-T-H-A.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they were still in Frankfurt?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were still in Frankfurt, right. And they had their apartment, which was also-- they also broke the glass on Kristallnacht. But he was able to, you know-- he put wood shutters there or something. And they were in that apartment.

speaker: interviewer
text: And waiting for an exit visa to England?

speaker: interviewee
text: Exactly. They were waiting for some kind of a family visa that was promised to them by some relatives that they had in London. And meanwhile, I-- you know, I tried to get out, and I--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any agencies or anybody that could help you at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a Jewish agency called [NON-ENGLISH]. And many of those people in the [NON-ENGLISH], the Jewish people, had left already. And there were few people left who would help others if they needed help.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did [NON-ENGLISH] mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: A cultural-- a cultural organization. In earlier years, they were something similar to, to, to a Jewish Y in America. They had all kinds-- they had performances, and they had exhibitions. But then, in time, they couldn't have that anymore. So they just were there to help others, to help them in every way they could, to help them get out, and find them a country to go to.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. When we do the next date, we'll find out if they were able to help you with anything.

speaker: interviewee
text: All right.
